A.content:
I didn't trust it at first. Another web3 platform claiming it "pays creators fairly." Heard that one before.

But then I read how @RallyOnChain works and something clicked. Most creator reward systems are black boxes. Someone decides who gets what and you never know why. Rally doesn't do that. Scoring runs through GenLayer's intelligent contracts. AI evaluates your content on originality, accuracy, how well you explain the project, whether real people engage. The weights are public.

I went to app.rally.fun, picked a campaign, wrote a post about what the protocol does, submitted. Tokens showed up on Base after the period. Not promises. Actual USDC and Rally Points, distributed on-chain.

What gets me is the no minimum followers thing. A creator with 300 followers writing something genuinely good can outscore someone with 50K posting generic shill content.

If you've spent hours making content for a project and got nothing while someone with 10x your followers copied the announcement and got paid, Rally was built for that frustration.

Anyone here tried submitting to a campaign yet? Curious how the scoring feels from the creator side.

B.content:
There's a question nobody asks about web3 marketing: how do you know the attention is real?

Not impressions. Not follower counts. Not someone Quote Tweeting with a fire emoji. Actual attention, someone who read the thing and engaged with it on their own.

@RallyOnChain built an answer to this. Their beta is live. Projects put up campaigns with prize pools on app.rally.fun. You pick one, write your post, submit. Then GenLayer's AI reads it. Not just checking if you tagged the right account. It evaluates whether your content explains the project accurately, whether it's original, whether it makes sense.

Rewards distributed on-chain. Base and zkSync Era. Stablecoins and Rally Points. Verifiable on explorer.

What struck me is the transparency. Every scoring dimension visible. Weights public. Compare that to every web3 "influencer deal" where you DM someone on Telegram and hope they send tokens.

The protocol itself matters more than the campaigns running on it. Verified attention markets with transparent AI scoring isn't a small thing.

Has anyone compared their Rally score across campaigns? Wondering if certain content types consistently score higher.

C.content:
Stumbled into Rally's beta last week and I've been weirdly obsessed since.

Not because of the rewards, although getting paid in USDC for writing about web3 projects you already care about isn't nothing. It's the scoring system.

@RallyOnChain runs on GenLayer and the way it evaluates submissions is different. You go to app.rally.fun, find a campaign, write your post about what the project does, hit submit. Then the AI grades you. Not just a "did you mention the keywords" check. It scores originality, accuracy, alignment with the brief, engagement potential. Real replies and retweets factor in.

The weights are all public. No hidden criteria, no "we'll evaluate at our discretion," no minimum follower gate. Your 200-follower account with good content can beat a 50K account posting generic stuff.

Tokens settle on Base after each period. Stablecoins plus Rally Points. On-chain, traceable, no middleman.

This is basically infrastructure. The kind of thing that seems obvious in retrospect but someone had to build it.

What I can't figure out is whether this makes more sense for small creators or big accounts. What's your read?

A2.content:
Spent six hours on a thread for a project I actually believe in. Got nothing. Not even a like from their team. Meanwhile someone with 50K followers copy-pasted the announcement and got paid. That exact frustration is why @RallyOnChain's beta just caught my attention.

Here's what's different. Rally runs on GenLayer. When you submit content, AI scores it on originality, accuracy, how well you explain the project, and whether real people engage. The weights are public. You can see them before writing a single word.

No minimum follower gate. A 300-follower account that writes something genuinely good can outscore a 50K account posting generic shill. That's not a theory, that's how the scoring works.

Rewards settle on Base after each period. USDC and Rally Points. On-chain, traceable. No "DM us your wallet and wait."

Rally's beta is live right now with real payouts. I've been reading through how the scoring actually works and the transparency angle alone should have every small creator paying attention.

If you've ever gotten ghosted by a project after doing real work, this is worth a look. Has anyone actually submitted to a campaign yet? Drop your score.

B2.content:
A 300-follower account just outscored a 50K account on a paid creator campaign.

That shouldn't be possible in web3. But @RallyOnChain's beta is live and that's literally how it works. GenLayer's AI reads your submission and scores it on whether your content actually explains the project, whether it's original, whether it makes sense.

I've been looking at how the scoring works. The weights are public. Every dimension visible before you write. No hidden criteria, no "we'll evaluate at our discretion," no gatekeepers.

Rewards hit your wallet on Base. USDC and Rally Points. On-chain, verifiable, no middleman.

This is live right now. Actual campaigns, actual payouts, actual AI scoring on content quality. Not testnet. Not promises.

The no minimum follower thing gets me. If you write something genuinely good, you can beat accounts 100x your size. That's never been possible before in crypto creator incentives.

I'm curious whether the AI scoring holds up at scale though. What happens when everyone starts optimizing for the algorithm? Does quality still win? Would love to hear from anyone who's actually submitted.

C2.content:
Web3 creator payouts are broken and @RallyOnChain just shipped the fix.

Here's the problem. You make real content for a project. Spend hours. Post it. What happens? Nothing. Some guy with bots gets the deal because his follower count looks good on a spreadsheet.

Rally's beta is live and it works differently. You go to app.rally.fun, pick a campaign, write a post that actually explains the project, submit. GenLayer's AI scores you on originality, accuracy, alignment, engagement. The weights are public. You see exactly what you're graded on before you start.

No follower minimum. Your 200-follower account with genuine content beats a 50K account posting garbage. That's the scoring model.

Payouts settle on Base. USDC and Rally Points. On-chain and traceable. No DMs, no waitlists, no "we'll be in touch."

This is live right now. Real campaigns running, real tokens moving, real AI evaluating whether your content is actually good or just has engagement farming vibes.

What I want to know: if this model catches on across web3, does having a large following become less valuable? Or does quality scoring just create a different kind of competition? Anyone here submitted yet?

B3.content:
A 300-follower account can outscore a 50K account on a paid creator campaign. Let that sink in.

That shouldn't be possible in web3. But @RallyOnChain's beta is live and that's exactly how it works. GenLayer's AI reads your submission and evaluates whether your content actually explains the project and whether it makes sense. Follower count doesn't factor in.

I went to app.rally.fun and read through the scoring weights before writing this. They're fully public. Every dimension visible before you start. No hidden criteria. No gatekeepers deciding behind closed doors.

Payouts settle on Base and zkSync in USDC. On-chain, verifiable, no middleman.

This is live right now with actual campaigns running and real tokens moving. Not testnet.

The part I keep coming back to is what happens when this scales. Right now the scoring feels fair. But what about when thousands of creators start optimizing their posts for the algorithm specifically? Does the AI adapt? Does genuine quality still win or does the system get gamed?

If you've submitted to a campaign I'd genuinely love to hear what your experience was like. Did the score feel fair compared to what you expected?

D4.content:
I wrote my first Rally submission trying to game the AI scoring.

Used every trick. Packed keywords, structured it like a winning template, front-loaded the project name. Figured I could reverse-engineer what the AI rewards and game the leaderboard.

Scored poorly.

@RallyOnChain runs on GenLayer and the scoring weights are public before you submit. I checked at app.rally.fun and the rubric was right there. Originality. Accuracy. How well you actually explain the project. Whether real people engage. The system already tells you what it wants.

So I wrote something genuine. Actually explained the protocol instead of keyword-stuffing. Score jumped. Not because I'm some genius, but because the AI rewards substance over gaming.

Payouts settle on-chain via Base and zkSync Era. USDC and Rally Points. No gatekeepers, no minimum followers. That part genuinely surprised me.

The beta is live with real tokens moving. I tried gaming it, it didn't work, and that's what actually convinced me this might be legit.

What I can't figure out: if people realize you can't game this, does it attract better creators or just filter out the lazy ones? Would love to hear from anyone who's submitted.
